Scheduler  
• Ultimate time-manager for your computer's processor  
• Decides which process/thread runs when  
• Policies:  
• CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) – The Default:  
• This is what Linux uses for most everyday tasks.  
• To give each process a "fair" share of the CPU.  
• It doesn't guarantee equal time slices, but equal runtime.  
• Processes that have been waiting longer get more CPU time when it's their turn.  
• Real time SCHED_FIFO/SCHED_RR  
CFS is not deterministic — if you need guaranteed deadlines, use RT scheduling classes.  
DEMO — Scheduling Effect  
(1/3)  
Goal: Show how OS scheduling mediates CPU access and affects timing.  
Linux enforces fairness even when tasks misbehave; bare metal assumes your code is perfect.  
Fairness vs Guarantees: Linux prioritizes fairness; it does not guarantee precise timing at normal priority.  
Start two hogs on the same core  
taskset  
set or retrieve the CPU affinity of a running process.  
Specifying coresa process is allowed to run on  
1. Open terminal and execute  
-c 0  
yes  
CPU core 0 to use  
$ taskset -c 0 yes > /dev/null &  
$ taskset -c 0 yes > /dev/null &  
Linux command that continuously outputs the string "y" to  
standard output. It's intentionally designed to run indefinitely,  
making it good for testing CPU affinity.  
This command starts a process that continuously uses  
CPU time (the yes command) and forces it to run  
exclusively on the first CPU core (core 0), while hiding  
its output from your screen. It runs this process in the  
background so you can continue using your terminal.  
>
It redirects the standard output of a command to a file.  
/dev/null  
a special file in Linux. Anything written to /dev/null is  
discarded. preventing it from cluttering your terminal  
&
tells the shell to run the command in the background.  
DEMO — Scheduling Effect  
(2/3)  
2. In another terminal, execute  
$ top  
The real time view of a running system (processes) will be shown as below:  
Observations  
• Look at %CPU and NI (nice).  
• Notice both hogs are around ~50% each—fair sharing.”  
DEMO — Scheduling Effect  
(3/3)  
Nice: Higher nice → lower priority (more positive number = more “polite”).  
Nice changes the weight of competition, not a hard cap. A niced task can still show 100% when no one else  
competes on that core.  
3. Reduce the priority of one process by making it more nice (more positive NI).”  
• In terminal 1 execute command  
288021 is the PID of one process  
$ renice +10 288021  
288021 (process ID) old priority 0, new priority 10  
Observations:  
• Notice NI changed to +10.  
• Normal yes (NI 0): ~90%  
• Niced yes (NI +10): ~10%  
• This is weighted fairness—not a hard cap. The lower-priority hog runs less often even though it’s ready to  
run all the time.  
Memory Management  
• Memory Management is how the operating system handles the computer's RAM.  
• It's responsible for  
• allocating memory to processes,  
• ensuring they don't interfere with each other  
• making efficient use of available resources.  
• Every process sees its own private virtual address space.  
Memory Management  
• The Problem  
• Physical RAM is limited.  
• Processes often require more memory than is physically available, or it’s beneficial to allow processes  
to use more memory than physically present.  
• The Solution: Virtual Memory  
• Creates an illusion for each process that it has a large, contiguous block of memory, even if the  
physical memory is fragmented and smaller.  
• This is done by using disk space as an extension of RAM.  
• How it works:  
• Processes address memory using virtual addresses.  
• The Memory Management Unit (MMU) translates these virtual addresses into physical addresses (the  
actual addresses in RAM).  
Virtual Address  
MMU  
Physical Address  
IPC (InterProcess Communication)  
• IPC = Coordination + Communication between processes.  
• Kernel manages shared resources.

easier to use

VFS (Virtual Filesystem)  
VFS is a major abstraction layer inside Linux kernel that provides unified API for all filesystems  
Why VFS exits:  
• different filesystems store metadata differently  
• But user space expects one consistent API  
Example: open() must work same on:  
• /home (ext4)  
• /sys (sysfs)  
• /proc (procfs)  
• USB drive (exFAT or FAT32)  
• Remote filesystem (NFS)  
• Memory filesystem (tmpfs)

Networking  
• The Linux networking subsystem (netstack) is one of the largest and most complex parts of Kernel.  
• This is the OSI-like stack inside Linux.  
• Socket API (TCP/UDP/raw), routing, firewall hooks

Device Drivers  
Device Driver is a piece of kernel code that controls hardware or emulates hardware like interfaces, exposing  
them to user space through standard kernel subsystem:  
VFS  
Networking  
Input subsystem  
Character/Block driver framework  
• Drivers run in kernel;  
• Drivers are NOT the user programs.

Subsystem  
(USB, PCI,  
NET, VFS,  
input, etc.)

Special Filesystems: /proc, /sys, /dev  
These three filesystems provide ways for user-space programs (like your applications) to interact with the  
kernel.  
/sys (sysfs)  
/dev  
/proc  
• The Device and Driver Model  
• The Device Node Gateway  
• The Virtual Process and  
• sysfs is created directly from  
Kernel Information Hub  
kernel objects  
contains kernel runtime  
information, not real files  
/proc – The Virtual Process and Kernel Information Hub  
/proc is a virtual filesystem, the files you see in /proc are not stored on your hard disk.  
• Examples:  
• /proc/cpuinfo: Detailed information about your CPU(s) – model, clock speed, features.  
• /proc/meminfo: Information about memory usage – total, free, cached, etc.  
• /proc/<pid>/status: Status of a specific process (replace <pid> with the process ID). Shows state,  
UID, GID, memory usage, etc.  
• /proc/<pid>/maps: Memory map of a process, showing the virtual memory regions it uses (very useful  
for understanding memory management).  
• /proc/interrupts: Shows the number of interrupts handled by each CPU.  
• Use Cases: System monitoring, debugging processes, gathering kernel statistics.  
/proc  
$ cd /proc  
$ ls  
You'll see a directory for each running process, plus some system-wide info files  
cat /proc/cpuinfo # Display CPU information  
less /proc/meminfo # View memory information (allows scrolling)  
/sys (sysfs) – The Device and Driver Model  
/sys (sysfs) represents the device and driver model of the Linux kernel. It’s a structured way to expose  
information about hardware devices and the drivers that control them.  
• Hierarchical Structure: Organized in a tree-like structure reflecting the device tree.  
• Attributes: Devices and drivers expose attributes as files within /sys. These attributes represent runtime  
configuration options and the current state of the device.  
• Safe Exposure: Drivers carefully control which attributes are exposed, preventing accidental or malicious  
modification of critical settings.  
• Examples:  
• /sys/class/gpio: Information about GPIO pins. enable/disable GPIOs, set their direction (input/output)  
using attributes within this directory.  
• /sys/class/net/<iface>: Information about network interfaces (replace <iface> with the interface  
name, e.g., eth0, wlan0). Statistics, MAC address, etc.  
• /sys/bus/i2c/devices/: Information about I2C devices.  
• Use Cases: Device configuration, monitoring device status, driver interaction.  
/sys  
$ cd /sys  
$ ls  
see directories like 'class', 'bus', 'devices'  
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/address # Display the MAC address of eth0  
/dev – The Device Node Gateway  
/dev contains device nodes. These are entry points for user-space programs to interact with kernel drivers.  
Each device node represents a physical or virtual device.  
• Character Devices: Provide a stream of bytes. Examples: serial ports (/dev/ttyS0), keyboard  
(/dev/input/event*). Access is typically unbuffered.  
• Block Devices: Provide access to block-sized data. Examples: hard drives (/dev/sda), SSDs  
(/dev/nvme0n1). Access is buffered.  
• Examples:  
• /dev/random, /dev/urandom: Random number generators.  
• /dev/null: The bit bucket (discards any data written to it).  
• /dev/sda1: The first partition on the first SATA drive.  
• Use Cases: Direct access to hardware, controlling devices, reading data from devices.  
/dev  
cd /dev  
ls  
• interact with devices in /dev using specialized commands or programs. Here are a few examples:  
• /dev/null: Discard output: echo "Hello" > /dev/null  
• /dev/random and /dev/urandom: Generate random numbers: cat /dev/random  
• /dev/sda1: Access a disk partition (requires appropriate permissions and tools like fdisk, mount): sudo  
mount /dev/sda1 /mnt  
• /dev/ttyS0: Serial port (use tools like minicom or screen): sudo minicom -s  
Seeing Inside vs. Entering the Kernel  
• /proc and /sys are how engineers “see inside” the kernel  
• provide read-only (mostly) access to kernel data and state.  
• Can observe what's happening, don't directly control the kernel through these filesystems (except for  
a few carefully controlled writable attributes in /sys).  
• /dev is how apps “enter” kernel drivers  
• Device nodes provide the interface for user-space programs to interact with kernel drivers.  
• can send commands to the driver, read data from the device, and write data to the device.  
Observe → /proc, /sys  
Interact → /dev
